sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, with month-specific anchors first where possible.

items.title: Pokemon Legends: Arceus
items.body: Pokemon becomes stranger, older and more open.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists January 28.

items.title: Horizon Forbidden West
items.body: Guerrilla's sequel shows Sony's cross-generation strategy at blockbuster scale.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot and Horizon sources list February 18.

items.title: Elden Ring
items.body: FromSoftware turns opaque challenge into open-world folklore.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists February 25.

items.title: Gran Turismo 7
items.body: Sony's racing museum returns with old-school collecting and modern service friction.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists March 4.

items.title: Kirby and the Forgotten Land
items.body: Kirby steps into 3D ruins and becomes quietly monumental.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists March 25.

items.title: The Quarry
items.body: Supermassive turns summer horror into a sofa-decision machine.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists June 10.

items.title: Stray
items.body: A cat, a backpack drone and a decaying city become a mid-year indie image.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists July 19.

items.title: Xenoblade Chronicles 3
items.body: Monolith Soft closes a grand Switch RPG arc.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot 2022 calendar lists July 29.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: Steam Deck begins shipping
items.body: Valve makes PC libraries portable and turns compatibility into a living database.
items.sourceNote: GameSpot lists February 25, 2022 launch plans.

items.title: Microsoft announces the Activision Blizzard deal
items.body: The year opens with a platform-scale acquisition story rather than just a release story.
items.sourceNote: Microsoft announced the deal January 18, 2022.

items.title: Subscriptions become a buying lens
items.body: Game Pass, PlayStation Plus tiers and day-one PC releases shape how players discuss value.
items.sourceNote: Platform context; check service specifics during human edit.

items.title: Cross-gen releases remain normal
items.body: PS4/Xbox One versions continue beside PS5/Series editions, stretching the console transition.
items.sourceNote: 2022 release calendar context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players discovered, argued about and shared the month.

items.title: Elden Ring becomes a shared map
items.body: Messages, build videos and boss clips turn the Lands Between into an online commons.

items.title: Steam Deck makes compatibility social
items.body: Players trade settings, Proton notes and battery compromises as part of ownership.

items.title: The acquisition discourse never leaves
items.body: Microsoft/Activision turns regulatory language into everyday console-war vocabulary.

items.title: Indie virality can be tiny and huge
items.body: Vampire Survivors shows how a small PC game can take over timelines without cinematic marketing.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading from the player side of recent history.

items.title: One game swallowed the spring
items.body: Elden Ring made discovery feel communal, punishing and weirdly generous.

items.title: Handheld PC play became plausible
items.body: Steam Deck made libraries feel physical again without becoming cartridges.

items.title: The industry felt enormous and fragile
items.body: Acquisitions, subscriptions and layoffs made the business visible behind the games.

items.title: Small games could still change behaviour
items.body: Vampire Survivors made minimal interaction feel like a whole new evening ritual.
